title: After Maduro: Human Trafficking and the Vulnerability of Venezuelan Migrants in Colombia
gist: Following the U.S. capture of Nicolas Maduro in January 2026, Venezuelan migrants in Colombia face heightened human-trafficking risk amid political instability, with trafficking functioning as a structured revenue stream for armed groups (ELN, Tren de Aragua, Clan del Golfo) and a mechanism that sustains state fragility on both sides of the border.

insights.claim: The U.S. captured Nicolas Maduro on January 3, 2026, but Venezuela's political transition remains fragile, with the Maduro regime continuing under Delcy Rodriguez and concerns over a prolonged or indefinite electoral process and slowed economic recovery.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A drawn-out, contested Venezuelan transition keeps regional instability and migration pressures elevated rather than resolving them; watch electoral timeline and ELN demobilization.
insights.locator: Following the U.S. capture of Nicolas Maduro

insights.claim: Venezuelan migrants in Colombia show no near-term intention of returning home and analysts expect human trafficking to stay stagnant rather than improve until migrants see economic stability in Venezuela.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: U.S. repatriation push is unlikely to reverse migrant flows soon; Colombia's migrant burden and associated instability persist regardless of regime change.
insights.quote: It's going to stay stagnant because people aren't returning until they know they will have some kind of future, economic stability. I don't see the situation improving, might not get worse but won't improve.
insights.locator: In March, journalist Hollie McKay visited Medellin

insights.claim: Colombia's three-phase post-capture plan focused on regularization, resources, and humanitarian response fails to outline concrete steps for trafficking prevention, leaving the country exposed if a sudden migrant surge occurs.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A policy gap means a migration surge could spike trafficking and destabilize a key U.S. regional ally; monitor Colombian government anti-TIP measures.

insights.claim: Over 7.9 million Venezuelans have fled the country since 2014, with 2.8 million in Colombia as of November 2025, making Colombia home to the third-largest refugee population in the world.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The sheer scale establishes a persistent structural driver of trafficking vulnerability and pressure on Colombian public services and labor markets.

insights.claim: Reported trafficking cases in Colombia rose from 75 in 2016 to 436 in 2024, with Venezuelan TIP victims nearly doubling from 2020 to 2021, showing a strong correlation between rising Venezuelan migration and trafficking.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Migration flows are a leading indicator of trafficking volume; surges in arrivals should be read as forward signals of rising TIP and instability.
insights.locator: Migration as a Driving Force of TIP

insights.claim: By the end of 2025, 67 percent of Venezuelans in Colombia had received legal status, and regularization measures like PEP and the 10-year Temporary Protected Status correlate with increased reporting of trafficking as victims gain confidence to come forward.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Rising reported cases may reflect better detection (regularization) rather than worsening conditions; interpret TIP statistics in light of legal-status policy changes.
insights.locator: Access to regularization has also correlated

insights.claim: Armed groups including the ELN, Tren de Aragua, and Clan del Golfo use trafficking not as opportunistic abuse but as a structured business model, paying monthly salaries and forcing migrants into illicit activities to repay debts.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Trafficking diversifies armed-group revenue beyond drugs, entrenching nonstate actors; counter-TIP becomes a security imperative, not just humanitarian.
insights.quote: These are methods that armed groups are using to settle debts...they pay monthly salaries to carry out this type of activity.

insights.claim: An ILO study estimates each person subjected to sexual exploitation generates over $27,000 in annual profit, totaling roughly $1.46 billion in aggregate revenue for trafficking organizations across the United States, Colombia, and Peru.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Quantifies the financial incentive sustaining trafficking, explaining why interdiction without disrupting the revenue model fails.
insights.locator: An International Labor Organization study reports

insights.claim: Illegal mining is closely intertwined with sex trafficking and forced labor in remote, weakly governed areas of Colombia and Venezuela, with state complicity in Venezuela making mineral exploitation a revenue source for both traffickers and corrupt officials.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Illegal mining zones are trafficking and instability hotspots; mineral supply chains touching these regions carry governance and ESG risk.
insights.locator: Illegal mining is an important source of revenue

insights.claim: Trafficking reinforces weak governance by funding bribes and expanding nonstate-actor influence, making TIP both a symptom and a mechanism that sustains state fragility on both sides of the Colombia-Venezuela border.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Frames trafficking as central to regional stability strategy; investors in the region should treat anti-TIP progress as a governance bellwether.
insights.locator: TIP is thus not only a symptom

insights.claim: While 90 percent of working-age Venezuelan migrants in Colombia are employed, only 18 percent work in their respective fields and the vast majority are in the informal sector without labor protections.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Informal-labor concentration creates a shadow market that empowers armed groups and undermines Colombia's labor-reform and formalization goals.

insights.claim: Impunity is severe: only 52 of 908 reported trafficking cases between 2011 and 2016 resulted in convictions, and as of 2023 there were no criminal prosecutions for labor-exploitation trafficking, with no law recognizing armed-group recruitment as trafficking.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Near-total impunity means legal frameworks exist on paper but offer little deterrence; justice-system reform is the bottleneck to reducing TIP.
